title: Myasthenic Crisis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Neurology, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize myasthenic crisis and distinguish from cholinergic crisis
Apply rapid treatment with IVIG or plasmapheresis

vignette.text:
A 52-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis presents with worsening dyspnea, dysphagia, and weakness over 3 days. She recently completed a course of azithromycin for pneumonia. She is now having difficulty speaking and is using accessory muscles.

Past Medical History: Myasthenia gravis (AChR antibody positive), s/p thymectomy

Medications: Pyridostigmine 60mg QID, prednisone 20mg daily

physicalExam.neuro: Ptosis bilateral, weak neck flexion, nasal speech, symmetric proximal weakness 3/5

questions.stem: This patient has worsening MG symptoms with respiratory compromise.
questions.question: What defines myasthenic crisis?

options.text: MYASTHENIC CRISIS: DEFINITION: Life-threatening weakness requiring mechanical ventilation or airway protection; OR imminent respiratory failure; CLINICAL FEATURES: Rapidly progressive weakness; Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, dysarthria, weak cough; Respiratory: dyspnea, tachypnea, accessory muscle use; FVC <15-20 mL/kg or NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O; TRIGGERS: Infection (most common - like this patient's pneumonia); Medications (aminoglycosides, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, Mg); Surgery; Pregnancy/postpartum; Tapering immunosuppression; DISTINGUISH FROM CHOLINERGIC CRISIS: Excess acetylcholinesterase inhibitors; SLUDGE symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis; Miosis, bradycardia, fasciculations
Any MG exacerbation is a crisis
Respiratory status is not relevant to definition
Medications never trigger crisis

questions.explanation: Myasthenic Crisis: Life-threatening weakness requiring ventilation or with imminent respiratory failure. Features: progressive weakness, bulbar symptoms, respiratory distress. TRIGGERS: Infection (most common), medications (aminoglycosides, macrolides like azithromycin, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers), surgery, pregnancy. Distinguish from cholinergic crisis (excess pyridostigmine) which has SLUDGE symptoms, miosis, bradycardia.

questions.question: When and how should you manage the airway?

options.text: AIRWAY MANAGEMENT IN MG CRISIS: INTUBATION CRITERIA: FVC <15 mL/kg (or <1 L); NIF weaker than -20 to -25 cmH2O; Declining FVC (>30% drop); Severe bulbar weakness with aspiration; Inability to clear secretions; Rising CO2 or respiratory fatigue; THIS PATIENT: FVC 0.8L = critical, needs intubation; INTUBATION CONSIDERATIONS: Use non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium) - may need lower dose; AVOID succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis due to reduced cholinesterase); Consider awake intubation if possible; Have difficult airway equipment ready; POST-INTUBATION: Hold pyridostigmine initially (reduces secretions); Can resume when improving; BiPAP may bridge if mild and improving
Wait for CO2 retention to intubate
Succinylcholine is preferred for RSI
FVC 0.8 L is adequate

questions.explanation: Airway in MG Crisis: Intubate for FVC <15 mL/kg (or <1L), NIF weaker than -20-25, declining FVC, severe bulbar weakness, aspiration risk. This patient with FVC 0.8L needs intubation. Use non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium) at reduced dose. AVOID succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis). Hold pyridostigmine initially (reduces secretions). Consider BiPAP only as bridge in mild cases.

questions.question: What is the treatment for myasthenic crisis?

options.text: MG CRISIS TREATMENT: IMMUNOTHERAPY (rapid acting): PLASMAPHERESIS: 5 exchanges over 10-14 days; Removes circulating antibodies; Faster onset than IVIG; Often preferred in crisis; OR IVIG: 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days; Equally effective long-term; May be easier to administer; CORTICOSTEROIDS: Chronic management, not acute crisis; May cause initial worsening ('steroid dip'); Start or increase after crisis stabilizes; HOLD PYRIDOSTIGMINE: Initially in intubated patients; Reduces secretions; Resume when extubated; TREAT TRIGGER: Antibiotics for infection (avoid harmful ones); Remove offending medication; LONG-TERM: Optimize maintenance therapy; Consider thymectomy if not done
High-dose steroids are first-line for crisis
Continue pyridostigmine at high doses
IVIG and PLEX should be combined

questions.explanation: MG Crisis Treatment: PLASMAPHERESIS (5 exchanges) OR IVIG (2g/kg over 2-5 days) - equally effective. PLEX often preferred for faster onset. HOLD pyridostigmine initially in intubated patients. Steroids for chronic management but may cause initial worsening - don't start in acute crisis. Treat underlying trigger. Do NOT combine PLEX and IVIG.

questions.stem: Her crisis was triggered by azithromycin.
questions.question: Which medications should be avoided in MG?

options.text: MEDICATIONS TO AVOID IN MG: ANTIBIOTICS: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin); Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin); MACROLIDES (azithromycin - like this patient); Tetracyclines; CARDIOVASCULAR: Beta-blockers; Calcium channel blockers (some); Procainamide, quinidine; NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKERS: Avoid if possible; If needed: reduced dose non-depolarizing; AVOID succinylcholine; OTHERS: Magnesium sulfate; Botulinum toxin; D-penicillamine; Quinine; Statins (rarely); SAFE ALTERNATIVES: Penicillins; Cephalosporins; Carbapenems; Vancomycin; Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
All antibiotics are safe
Beta-blockers are safe
Magnesium is safe in MG

questions.explanation: Medications to Avoid in MG: ANTIBIOTICS: Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides (like azithromycin - this patient's trigger), tetracyclines. CARDIAC: Beta-blockers, some CCBs. OTHERS: Magnesium, botulinum toxin, D-penicillamine. SAFE antibiotics: Penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems, vancomycin, TMP-SMX.

questions.question: What is the complete management?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE CRISIS: FVC 0.8L (<15 mL/kg), bulbar symptoms, respiratory distress after macrolide antibiotic trigger; (2) INTUBATE: FVC critically low; Use rocuronium (avoid succinylcholine); (3) HOLD PYRIDOSTIGMINE (reduces secretions while intubated); (4) START PLASMAPHERESIS: 5 exchanges over 10-14 days; OR IVIG 2g/kg over 2-5 days; (5) TREAT INFECTION: Use SAFE antibiotics (cephalosporin, not fluoroquinolone/macrolide); (6) SUPPORTIVE: DVT prophylaxis, nutrition, PT; (7) RESUME pyridostigmine when extubating; (8) OPTIMIZE long-term therapy after crisis resolves; (9) EDUCATE patient on triggers and medication avoidance
Start high-dose steroids immediately
Use fluoroquinolone for pneumonia
Wait for improvement before PLEX

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: Recognize crisis (FVC <15 mL/kg, bulbar symptoms, triggered by macrolide). Intubate using rocuronium. Hold pyridostigmine while intubated. Start PLEX or IVIG immediately. Treat infection with SAFE antibiotics (avoid macrolides, fluoroquinolones). Supportive care. Resume pyridostigmine when extubating. Optimize long-term therapy and educate on triggers.
